coredata.dc:title: Inner filter effect as a sensitive sensing platform for detection of nitrofurantoin using luminescent drug-based carbon nanodots
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Al-Hashimi, Baraa R.
Omer, Khalid M.
Rahman, Heshu S.
Othman, Hemn H.
coredata.dc:description: In the present paper, a sensitive and selective inner filter effect sensing platform was designed to detect nitrofurantoin (NIT) in pharmaceutical dosage form. Nitrogen and phosphorus co-doped carbon nanodots (CNDs) prepared via solvothermal treatment of folic acid and phosphoric acid. The prepared CNDs exhibit greenish fluorescence at 470 nm when excited at 340 nm with fluorescence quantum yield up to 40%. The CNDs exhibit high stability in various pH, temperature, and ionic strength which adds valuable merits to its pharmaceutical applications. The emission is quenched in the presence of absorber (here NIT) while the fluorophores were not quench by the presence of common pharmaceutical excipients. A fluorometric assay was fabricated to determine NIT in capsules by quenching of the CNDs. The linear response for the proposed method was from 5.0 μM to 90 μM with the detection limit being 1.4 μM. To validate the method, the recovery of NIT in spiked sample was measured which was 96.6% -103.3%. The method was applied to the determination of NIT in pharmaceutical capsule samples, with comparable results of a reference method stated by the British Pharmacopeia (BP). Furthermore, the sub-acute toxicity studies of CNDs were investigated using normal male Balb/c mice forcefully drunk with 3 different dosages of CNDs. Animals did not produce treatment related signs of toxicity or mortality in any of the animals tested during the 28 days observation period. Additionally, no significant (P > 0.05) changes in the body weight, haematological and biochemical parameters compared with the control group were not revealed. Similarly, histopathological examination of the internal vital organs did not show any morphological alterations.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrofurantoin
Carbon nanodots
Inner filter effect
Fluorescence sensor
Cytotoxicity of carbon dots, pharmaceutical probe

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Clinical manners and body weight assessment

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A scheme illustrating the synthesis of carbon nanodots and quenching by addition of nitrofurantoin that can be attributed to overlap of the CNDs excitation spectra and NIT absorption spectra.PL: photoluminescence.
A. TEM image, B. XRD pattern, C. FTIR spectra, and D. Raman spectra of CNDs.
High resolution XPS spectra of CNDs; A. Survey spectra of CNDs, and B–E. XPS spectra for all elements in the CNDs.

A. Absorption (black line), Excitation (red line), and emission (blue) spectra free CNDs (inset: photograph of aqueous solution of CNDs under visible light (left) and UV light (right)) B. Emission spectra of the CNDs at various excitation wavelengths.
The effect of pH (A), ionic strength (B), temperature (C), and excipients (D) on the fluorescence intensity of CNDs.

A. Fluorescence spectra of CNDs with the addition of different concentrations of nitrofurantoin. B. Calibration graph for CNDs with various concentrations of nitrofurantoin. C. Visual comparison of the effect of adding more nitrofurantoin to the CNDs.

Liver of male Balb/c mice after oral treatment with (A) normal saline (control negative) and CNDs at concentrations (B) 5.0 mg/kg, (C) 50 mg/kg, and (D) 300 mg/kg for 28 days. No signs of toxicity were found in the liver tissues of mice compared to control group (400× magnification).

The kidney of male Balb/c mice after oral treatment with (A) normal saline (control negative) and CNDs at concentrations (B) 5.0 mg/kg, (C) 50 mg/kg, and (D) 300 mg/kg for 28 days. No signs of toxicity were found in the renal tissues of mice compared to control group (400× magnification).
Determination of nitrofurantoin in capsules using CNDs as probe (No. of samples = 5).
Determination of nitrofurantoin using different methods.
Body weight of male Balb/C mice received normal saline and different doses of CNDs.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Values are mean ± SD (n = 6) and have been analysed using post hoc comparison test one way ANOVA. Data revealed non-significant (P > 0.05) difference in body weight after 4 weeks treatment. Low dose (LD: 5.0 mg/kg CNDs), medium dose (MD: 50 mg/kg CNDs), and high dose (HD: 300 mg/kg CNDs).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Liver and kidney function tests of male Balb/C mice received normal saline and different doses of CNDs for 4 weeks.
Haemogram results of male Balb/C mice received normal saline and various doses of CNDs for 4 weeks.

head.ce:title: Inner filter effect as a sensitive sensing platform for detection of nitrofurantoin using luminescent drug-based carbon nanodots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmacology and Toxicology, College of Pharmacy, University of Sulaimani, Sulaymaniyah City, Kurdistan Region, Iraq

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Pharmacology and Toxicology, College of Pharmacy

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Pharmacology and Toxicology, College of Pharmacy, University of Sulaimani, Sulaymaniyah City, Kurdistan Region, Iraq

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In the present paper, a sensitive and selective inner filter effect sensing platform was designed to detect nitrofurantoin (NIT) in pharmaceutical dosage form. Nitrogen and phosphorus co-doped carbon nanodots (CNDs) prepared via solvothermal treatment of folic acid and phosphoric acid. The prepared CNDs exhibit greenish fluorescence at 470 nm when excited at 340 nm with fluorescence quantum yield up to 40%. The CNDs exhibit high stability in various pH, temperature, and ionic strength which adds valuable merits to its pharmaceutical applications. The emission is quenched in the presence of absorber (here NIT) while the fluorophores were not quench by the presence of common pharmaceutical excipients. A fluorometric assay was fabricated to determine NIT in capsules by quenching of the CNDs. The linear response for the proposed method was from 5.0 μM to 90 μM with the detection limit being 1.4 μM. To validate the method, the recovery of NIT in spiked sample was measured which was 96.6% -103.3%. The method was applied to the determination of NIT in pharmaceutical capsule samples, with comparable results of a reference method stated by the British Pharmacopeia (BP). Furthermore, the sub-acute toxicity studies of CNDs were investigated using normal male Balb/c mice forcefully drunk with 3 different dosages of CNDs. Animals did not produce treatment related signs of toxicity or mortality in any of the animals tested during the 28 days observation period. Additionally, no significant (P > 0.05) changes in the body weight, haematological and biochemical parameters compared with the control group were not revealed. Similarly, histopathological examination of the internal vital organs did not show any morphological alterations.

ce:keyword.ce:text: Cytotoxicity of carbon dots, pharmaceutical probe

ce:section.ce:para: Urinary tract infections (UTI) are amongst the most common infections worldwide affecting around 150 million individuals per year [1]. It can be caused by both gram positive and gram negative bacteria with the most common causative agent being Escherichia coli resulting in about 80% of the cases [2]. Symptomatic urinary tract infections require antimicrobial therapy which involves many drugs, one of the most effective ones is nitrofurantoin (NIT) [3]. NIT is an antibacterial agent that was first sold in 1953 as capsules to be administered orally [4]. This drug is prescribed specifically for uncomplicated UTI and it is required to be reduced to its active form by susceptible bacteria after which it acts by damaging bacterial DNA and RNA [5]. It is registered on the World Health Organization list of essential medicines as one of the most effective and safe medications in the health system [6].

Currently, there are many methods used to determine NIT in pharmaceutical dosage forms such as high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [7], Flow Injection Analysis [8], Square wave cathodic adsorptive stripping voltammetry [9] and metal organic frameworks (MOFs) [10]. Although these methods can afford high selectivity and sensitivity; but they have many drawbacks such as expensive instrumentation, high running costs, sample pre-treatment and long processing time [11–14]. Thus, one should develop new methods that offer simplicity, sensitivity, selectivity and time effectiveness.

Carbon nanomaterial including fullerenes, nanotubes, graphene and CNDs has attracted tremendous interest in research over the past years [15]. CNDs have many advantages over all other types of carbon nanomaterials due to their biocompatibility, high stability and ease of synthesis [16–19]. Consequently, CNDs showed great potential in applications like bio-sensing, bio-imaging, drug delivery and fluorescent probes [20–25]. Using carbon dots as probes for detection and quantification of active pharmaceutical ingredients in drugs is one of its most popular applications of in the pharmaceutical field [26,27].

Inner filter effect (IFE) was regarded as an interference problem years ago, but recently it has been used as an effective strategy in sensing and quantification of chemicals [28]. IFE is a non-radiation energy conversion model that can happen due to overlap of the absorber absorption band with the fluorophore band of excitation, emission or both [29]. The first mode of IFE is when the overlap occurs between the absorption spectrum of the absorber and excitation spectrum of the fluorophore [30]. The second mode IFE occurs when the absorption spectrum of the absorber and emission spectrum of the fluorophore overlap [31]. Carbon nanodots-based inner filter effect probes have been extensively used for sensing ions, small molecules and macromolecules [32]. Detection of ions is of particular interest in the fields of biological and environmental toxicology which includes copper, lead, cadmium and mercury [33–35]. Small molecules that are sensed by inner filter effect involve drugs like antibiotics, anticancer agents, insecticides, fungicides and pesticides [36–39]. Macromolecules detected by IFE mostly involve proteins like immunoglobulin, haemoglobin and several enzymes [40–42].In this study, we provide simple, rapid and low-cost precursor method with high selectivity and sensitivity to determine the content of NIT in pharmaceutical preparations. The proposed procedure involves synthesis of highly fluorescent carbon dots using one step hydrothermal method with folic acid being the carbon source and phosphoric acid acting as a solvent and as a doping agent (Fig. 1 ). Adding NIT to the CNDs solution would lead to effective quenching based on IFE sensing which can be used to assay its content in capsules; this is observed over a wide linear range with low detection limit. Moreover, the safety of the CNDs were evaluated by conducting in vivo studies on lab rats to investigate their biocompatibility and suitability for further applications in the pharmaceutical field.

Pure NIT was purchased from Solarbio Life Sciences, China. Folic acid, Phosphoric acid and sodium hydroxide were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich, Germany. NIT capsules were purchased from a local pharmacy. All chemicals used were analytical grade and highly purified. Deionized water (18 MΩ) was utilized through the whole work.

A single step solvothermal method was used for the preparation of the CNDs. In brief, 1.0 g folic acid was dissolved with 10.0 mL of concentrated phosphoric acid to form a clear solution. Then, this solution was placed in a 25 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave reactor and transferred to an oven where it was heated constantly for 4 h at 180 °C. The obtained solution was filtered using 0.22 μm filter membrane after the autoclave was cooled down. The pH of this solution was neutralized by adding sodium hydroxide pellets to it, filtered through 0.22 μm filter membrane then centrifuged at 12000 rpm for 15 min. Finally, the obtained CNDs were purified via dialyzing using dialysis membrane (1000 MWCO) against deionized water for 5 h. The final yellowish solution was stored in ambient temperature for further use.

Transmission electron microscope TECNAI G2 F20 microscope (Ames Lab, USA) at 200 kV was used to take TEM images to determine the morphological features and size of particles. The sample preparation involved placing a droplet of the CNDs solution on a copper supported grid then leaving it to dry. Similarly, Thermo Escalab 250 XI X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Scientific, USA) was used to generate the XPS data with the survey scan binding energy being 1486.6 eV at 1.0 eV intervals while the data were deconvoluted using CasaXPS® (Ver. 2.3.22). Subsequently, a Cary 60 Spectrophotometer (Agilent Technologies, USA) was used to obtain the UV–Vis absorption spectra. Fluorescence spectra were recorded via Cary Eclipse Fluorescence Spectrophotometer (Agilent Technologies, USA), both the emission and excitations slits were set at 5.0 nm. Empyrean X-ray diffractometer (PANalytical, Netherland) was used to collect the X-ray diffraction patterns XRD. The functional groups of the CNDs was detected by Fourier transform infrared spectrometer FTIR Nicolet iS50 (Thermo Scientific, USA) through a diamond ATR attachment. The Raman spectra were produced using inVia Reflex (Renishaw, UK) with Nd:YAG laser source at 785 nm (energy output 50 mW). The scanning range was from 500 to 3000 cm−1 with a spectral resolution of 2.0 cm−1.

The content of 20 NIT capsules was collected and placed into a mortar which was then crushed to remove the coat of the constituting pellets. The stock solution of 1 ∗ 10−3 M NIT was prepared by dissolving the powder of NIT in DMF followed by filtration through 0.22 μm filter membrane to eliminate any remaining un-dissolved particles. Finally, with deionized water was used to dilute the obtained solution and stored for additional processes.

The quantitative determination of NIT was performed at 25 °C by mixing 2.0 mL of 0.02 mg/mL CNDs solution with 2.0 mL the NIT solution and the pH was set at approximately 3 using an acetate buffer. Then 1.0 minute mixing with vortex was used to ensure homogenization after which the fluorescence emission spectra were recorded.

To examine the thermo-stability of CNDs, The CNDs solution were heated over a range of 25 to 100 °C for 10 min, then fluorescence spectra of the solutions was measured directly. The effect of ionic strength was investigated using various concentrations of NaCl from 0.1 M to 2.0 M NaCl. Furthermore, pH effect was studied from 2 to 12 using HCl and NaOH to adjust the pH of the medium.

The fluorescence quantum yield of the prepared CNDs was determined by comparing it to the standard fluorescein (QY = 95%) while using 0.1 M sodium hydroxide as solvent [43]. The following equation was used to determine the QY:
ϕ X
=
ϕ S
F X
⋅
A S
⋅
η 2
F S
⋅
A X
⋅
η S
2

The subscripts “S” and “X” refer to the standard and sample respectively. While ɸ stands for the quantum yield, F stands for the area under the curve of the fluorescence spectra, A is the absorbance and ɳ is the solvent refractive index.

Adult male Balb/c mice aging 8–10 weeks and weighing 22–25 g were procured from the Animal House of College of Educational Science, Department of Biology, University of Sulaimani. Upon arrival of the animals to the laboratory, they were amended to the workroom condition of ambient temperature (25 ± 1.0 °C) and relative humidity (70%) with the equivalent time of regular light-dark cycle for 5 days in straw wood bedding plastic polypropylene cages with free access to ordinary tap water and regular mice pellets. The Ethical approval for performing this experiment was gained at the College of Medicine, University of Sulaimani.

Twenty four mice were assigned subjectively into 4 groups of 6 mice each. The 1st group displayed control negative and received normal saline, while group 2, 3, and 4 displayed low dose (LD: 5.0 mg/kg CNDs), medium dose (MD: 50 mg/kg CNDs), and high dose (HD: 300 mg/kg CNDs), respectively. Animals were deprived from eating but not drinking for at least 12 h before constant dosing in the early morning. Therapies were given to mice via forceful, tip balled, stainless steel needle at a dose of 0.1 mL per 10 g body weight once a day exactly for 4 consecutive weeks [44,45].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Clinical manners and body weight assessment
ce:section.ce:para: Experimental mice in all groups were observed for behavioural abnormalities, toxicological signs, and diet consumption two times per day during the experimental period. Body weight of each animal was measured on day 0, 14, and 28 by the Sartorius digital balance.

At the end of the study and after sacrificing of the animals, approximately 1.0 mL of blood were obtained from posterior venacava under general anaesthesia and was collected in tubes with EDTA, mixed well and gently, then used for haemogram parameters including total red blood cells (RBC), haemoglobin (Hb), packed cell volume (PCV), total white blood cells (WBC), and platelets (PLT) by automatic haematology analyser (Swelab Alpha Plus, Sweden). Blood samples also withdrawn into tubes without anticoagulant for serum collection by centrifuging at 2500 rpm for 10 min and conducting blood biochemistry including aspartate aminotransferase (AST), alkaline phosphatase (ALP), alanine aminotransferase (ALT), urea, and creatinine using optimized diagnostic kits (Roche Diagnostic GmbH, Indianapolis, USA) and analysed using automatic biochemistry analyser (Hitachi LTD, Chiyoda, Japan).

Directly after animal sacrificing under general anaesthesia using a mixture of xylazine and ketamine, hepatic and renal specimens were excised, rinsed with normal saline to remove the blood clot and debris, sliced into small parts and put in freshly prepared formalin (10%, v/v) solution for at least 2 days for fixation before transporting into the Histopathology laboratory. Later on, tissue sections were transported into a plastic cassette and then into an automated tissue processer for regular dehydration, cleaning, impregnation, and infiltration. After that, processed sections were inserted in paraffin wax to obtain blocks. Subsequently, semi-automated microtome was used for tissue trimming and sections at 5 μm were attached on glass slides using a hot plate. Then, slides were rehydrated progressively using ethanol at altered concentrations. Next, normal tap water was used for final washing and then stained with Harris's haematoxylin and eosin (H & E) to be tested with a normal light microscope at a power 400× magnification (Leica, Germany).

In order to describe the morphology and size of the CNDs, transmission electron microscope (TEM) was used. The TEM image (Fig. 2A) shows that the CNDs were spherical in shape with the average size around 5.0 nm. The optical properties of the CNDs can be linked to their size as particles with a diameter of 5–10 nm tend to have high quantum yield [46]. The X-rays diffraction pattern (XRD) shown in Fig. 2B exhibits a broad peak at 2θ = 24.4 Å, this peak emerges from the disordered carbon dots [47].

Functional groups of the CNDs were investigated using Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR) as shown in Fig. 2C. The broad peak observed at 3750–2800 cm−1 originates from OH and NH bands which is in correlation with the good water solubility of the CNDs [48,49]. The absorption bands at 1715 cm−1 and 1588 cm−1 can be attributed to CO and CC stretching respectively [50,51]. The absorption peak at 1588 cm−1 can be related to OH bending [52]. Raman spectra (Fig. 2D) show a weak band at 1080 cm−1 which is due to CO stretching vibration, and it shows a strong band at 900 cm−1 which is related to CC stretching [53,54]. Typical graphite features are observed at 1360 cm−1 and at 1630 cm−1 which parallel to D band and G band, respectively [55,56].

To further characterize the composition and functional groups of the carbon dots, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was employed. The XPS survey spectra (Fig. 3A) displayed 4 main peaks: C 1s (285 eV), N 1s (402 eV), O 1s (532 eV), P 2p (135 eV). The presence of nitrogen and phosphorus peaks indicates that the carbon dots are nitrogen and phosphorus doped. The high resolution XPS spectra can be used to further deconvolute the C 1s to give four main peaks (Fig. 3B): CO at 286.8 eV, C = N at 285.7 eV, CC at 284.4 eV and CO at 285.3 eV [57,58]. The O 1s peak (Fig. 3C) shows 3 main resolved peaks: (CO, 533.3 eV), phosphorus oxide (PO, 531.2 eV) and Carbonyl (CO, 532.5 eV) [59,60]. The N 1s band (Fig. 3D) is deconvoluted into three peaks: NH, 402.6 eV, C-NH, 400.1 eV and CN, 402.1 eV [61,62]. Finally, the P 2p spectra (Fig. 3E) can be convoluted to two peaks: PO 2p3/2, 133.1 eV and PO 2p1/2, 130.1 eV [63,64]. Consequently, the XPS data results are in complete agreement with the FTIR and Raman results.

The optical characteristics of the CNDs were studied using UV–Vis absorption spectra, fluorescence excitation and emission spectra (Fig. 4A). The UV- Vis absorption spectra show a peak at 235 nm that can be related to π to π* transition of the CC bonds [65,66]. Another peak is observed at 337 nm which can be attributed to n to π* of the CO bond [67].

The highest emission in the fluorescence spectra was observed at 470 nm with the excitation wavelength being 340 nm (Fig. 4B). There was slight change in the position of emission peak upon changing the excitation wavelength which indicates that the CNDs exhibit very slight wavelength dependent fluorescence emissions. When the CNDs were excited at 300 nm, it showed maximum emission peak at 472 nm, while when excited at 440 nm the maximum emission peak was observed at 484 nm. This slight excitation dependent fluorescence emission can be attributed to the presence of multiple emission centers within the CNDs and can also originate from the slight differences in the shape and size of the particles which leads to slight changes in the energy band gap [68,69].

The stability of the CNDs was studied by measuring the variation of fluorescence intensity in different condition such as pH, temperature, and ionic strength. The fluorescence intensity remained almost constant over a wide range of pH from 4.0 to 10.0; however, it drops significantly in extreme acidic or basic pH that can be attributed to the protonation and deprotonation significantly of the surface functional groups as shown in Fig. 5A [70]. The CNDs showed high ionic strength stability while being placed in various solutions of NaCl having a concentration of up to 2.0 M (Fig. 5B). The CNDs demonstrated high thermal stability as it was very stable even at 100 °C for 10 min (Fig. 5C) which can be related to the presence of hydroxyl and carboxyl groups on their surface that can insulate the fluorescence centers and resist degradation due to heating by stabilizing particle aggregation [71]. The long-range of pH working, stability in extreme conditions of temperature and ionic strength make the CNDs and the fluorometric assay rigid and robust, which is highly desirable in pharmaceutical analysis.

The matrix effect resulting from pharmaceutical excipients was studied by adding aliquots of high concentration of these additives to the CNDs solution and monitoring fluorescence intensity. Excipient like lactose, l-ascorbic acid, talc, magnesium tartrate, and starch were used for the interference study. There was almost no change in the fluorescence intensity upon the addition of the excipients indicating that there is no interference originating from the additives which in turn increases the credibility of the proposed analytical method (Fig. 5D).

The excitation and emission spectra of the CNDs were analysed as well as the absorption spectra of NIT in order to deduce the potential mechanism of quenching (Fig. 4A). Direct overlap of the NIT absorption spectra with the CNDs excitation spectra is observed while it partially overlaps with the CNDs emission spectra. This overlap can reduce the photoluminescence intensity as NIT can absorb radiation preventing it from reaching the CNDs [72]. As a result, a combination of both primary and secondary inner filter affects is potential quenching mechanisms [73,74]. Furthermore, the UV spectra for the mixture of the CNDs and nitrofurantoin were recorded to evaluate the possibility of static quenching (Supplementary information, Fig. S1). The data revealed that the UV spectra of the combination are a summation of each individual spectrum of CNDs and nitrofurantoin which refutes the possibility of static quenching [75].

The excitation and emission spectra of the CNDs were analysed as well as the absorption spectra of NIT in order to deduce the potential mechanism of quenching (Fig. 4A). Direct overlap of the NIT absorption spectra with the CNDs excitation spectra is observed while it partially overlaps with the CNDs emission spectra. This overlap can reduce the photoluminescence intensity as NIT can absorb radiation preventing it from reaching the CNDs [72]. As a result, a combination of both primary and secondary inner filter affects is potential quenching mechanisms [73,74]. Furthermore, the UV spectra for the mixture of the CNDs and nitrofurantoin were recorded to evaluate the possibility of static quenching (Supplementary information, Fig. S1). The data revealed that the UV spectra of the combination are a summation of each individual spectrum of CNDs and nitrofurantoin which refutes the possibility of static quenching [75].

Animals in all treatment groups displayed no signs of toxicity such as self-segregation, recumbency, shedding hair, tremor, diarrhoea, salivation, coma or erratic behaviours such as anorexia, fierceness, lethargy, nervousness, and cannibalism. Simultaneously, non-significant (P > 0.05) body weight increment was reported in all treated animals when compared to untreated control group (Table 3 ) which means CNDs were not negatively affecting the weight gain ability of the animals.

There was no potential toxicity or harm to the cell structure of liver and kidneys and there was no corruption, incendiary response, fibrosis or neighbourhood greasy degeneration in liver and kidneys. In addition, the histopathological analysis of the hepatic tissues of treated animals showed no dilatation of central vein and adjacent hepatocytes, as well as no infiltration of inflammatory cells, no congestion in the portal veins and hepatic sinusoids, no degenerative changes, necrosis and apoptosis of hepatocytes were observed (Fig. 7 ).

Furthermore, there was no histopathological change in the renal tissues of animals from three treated groups compared to that of control group. The glomeruli tuft demonstrated normal structure without congestion, dilatation, swelling, apoptosis, and vacuolization. Additionally, nephrons exhibited typical and visible nucleoli without degeneration and dilatation. Renal cast formation in the tubular lumen and cystic tubular dilatation also were not exhibited (Fig. 8 ). Thus, the experiment recommends that the lethal dose of CNDs value in investigated mice might be more than 300 mg/kg.

Values are mean ± SD (n = 6) and have been analysed using post hoc comparison test one way ANOVA. Data showed non-significant (P > 0.05) differences in hepatic and renal function tests after 28 days of treatment when compared to that of control group. ALP: alkaline phosphatase, ALT: alanine aminotransferase, and AST: aspartate aminotransferase. Low dose (LD: 5.0 mg/kg CNDs), medium dose (MD: 50 mg/kg CNDs), and high dose (HD: 300 mg/kg CNDs).

Values are mean ± SD (n = 6) and have been analysed using post hoc comparison test one way ANOVA. Data showed non-significant (P > 0.05) differences in haematological parameters after 28 days of treatment when compared to that of control group. RBC: red blood cell, Hb: haemoglobin, PCV: packed cell volume, PLT: platelet, and WBC: white blood cell. Low dose (LD: 5.0 mg/kg CNDs), medium dose (MD: 50 mg/kg CNDs), and high dose (HD: 300 mg/kg CNDs).

In summary, nitrogen and phosphorus co-doped carbon nanodots were prepared from folic acid using a simple single step solvothermal method that resulted in spherically shaped particles with a diameter of around 5 nm. The CNDs are demonstrating high stability in various extreme temperatures (up to 100 °C), pH, ionic strengths (up to 2.0 M) and matrix effect (pharmaceutical excipients) The CNDs showed green colored fluorescence with high quantum yield of approximately 40%. CNDs were quenched after addition of NIT due to IFE that results mostly from the overlap of NIT absorption band with the excitation band of the CNDs (primary IFE). Moreover, the minor overlap of NIT absorption band with the emission band of the CNDs also resulted in quenching (secondary IFE) but to a lesser extent. The proposed method provides advantages of being rapid, cheap and sensitive with a detection limit as low as 1.4 μM. Animals in all tested groups did not show any signs of erratic behaviour or physical signs of toxicity during the entire study period. The in vivo cytotoxic, haematological, and biochemical results confirmed that CNDs did not deliver any critically dangerous impact in treated mice at 3 different dosages. Subsequently, the carbon nanodots can be employed for loading drugs in pharmaceutical preparations, bio imaging as well as using as a biocompatible and safe probe.

The following are the supplementary data related to this article.
Fig. S1
Absorption of CNDs (black line), Absorption of Nitrofurantoin (blue line) and absorption spectra of the mixture of CNDs and nitrofuranoin (red line).
Fig. S1
Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2020.118835.

ce:list-item.ce:para: Baraa R. Al-Hashimi: Conducting the experiments, Writing - original draft, Data curation
Khalid M. Omer: Writing review & editing and Supervision
Heshu S. Rahman: Cytotoxicity experiments, Supervision, Writing review & editing
Hemn H. Othman: Hematology experiments and data analysis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that there are no conflicts of interest with this manuscript.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to thank the head and staff of the Department of Chemistry, College of Science, University of Sulaimani for their support in completion of this research.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Urinary tract infections: epidemiology, mechanisms of infection and treatment options

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. L. Flores-Mireles, J. N. Walker, M. Caparon, and S. J. Hultgren, “Urinary tract infections: Epidemiology, mechanisms of infection and treatment options,” Nat. Rev. Microbiol., vol. 13, no. 5, pp. 269–284, 2015.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Urinary tract infection syndromes. Occurrence, recurrence, bacteriology, risk factors, and disease burden

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: B. Foxman, “Urinary tract infection syndromes. Occurrence, recurrence, bacteriology, risk factors, and disease burden,” Infect. Dis. Clin. North Am., vol. 28, no. 1, pp. 1–13, 2014.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Other antimicrobials of interest in the era of extended-spectrum β-lactamases: fosfomycin, nitrofurantoin and tigecycline

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. He, H. Li, G. Mohr, B. Kovács, T. Werner, and O. S. Wolfbeis, “Novel Type of Ion-Selective Fluorosensor Based on the Inner Filter Effect: An Optrode for Potassium,” Anal. Chem., vol. 65, no. 2, pp. 123–127, 1993.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Inner filter effect (IFE) as a simple and selective sensing platform for detection of tetracycline using milk-based nitrogen-doped carbon nanodots as fluorescence probe

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: B. Al-Hashimi, K. M. Omer, and H. S. Rahman, “Inner filter effect (IFE) as a simple and selective sensing platform for detection of tetracycline using milk-based nitrogen-doped carbon nanodots as fluorescence probe,” Arab. J. Chem., vol. 13, no. 4, pp. 5151–5159, 2020.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Novel thermal quenching characteristics of luminescent carbon nanodots via tailoring the surface chemical groups

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R. Maiti, S. Mukherjee, S. Haldar, D. Bhowmick, and S. K. Ray, “Novel thermal quenching characteristics of luminescent carbon nanodots via tailoring the surface chemical groups,” Carbon N. Y., vol. 104, pp. 226–232, 2016.
M. Towers, “British Pharmacopoeia 2009,” Br. Pharmacopia, p. PC version, 2009.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Determination of related substances in nitrofurantoin by HPLC

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: G. Z.-Q. SUN Tao, “Determination of Related Substances in Nitrofurantoin by HPLC,” Chinese J. Pharm., vol. 1, no. 34, pp. 28–29, 2003.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Spectrophotometric determination of ezetimibe using MBTH reagent in pharmaceutical dosage form

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Shravya, R. S. Chandan, B. M. Gurupadayya, M. Sireesha, S. Adeunutula, and M. Pharm, “Spectrophotometric Determination of Ezetimibe Using Mbth Reagent in Pharmaceutical Dosage Form,” Int. J. Res. Ayurveda Pharm., vol. 2, no. 2, pp. 521–525, 2011.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Rapid determination of trace nitrofurantoin in cosmetics by surface enhanced Raman spectroscopy using nanoarrayed hydroxyl polystyrene-based substrate

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Zhang et al., “Rapid determination of trace nitrofurantoin in cosmetics by surface enhanced Raman spectroscopy using nanoarrayed hydroxyl polystyrene-based substrate,” J. Raman Spectrosc., vol. 50, no. 8, pp. 1094–1102, 2019.
